key_insight: A dual-domain ResNet18 model can identify crystallographic surface handedness, with reciprocal-space Fermi surface projections encoding chirality far more clearly than real-space atomic surface models.
engineering_relevance: This enables faster, more reliable chirality screening for catalytic, sensing, and spintronic surfaces, and suggests momentum-space imaging may be the most practical route for automated chiral surface characterization.
method: Fine-tuned ResNet18 classifiers on labeled atomic-structure images and simulated Fermi-surface projection maps.
Compared performance across real-space and reciprocal-space representations of high-Miller-index chiral metal surfaces.
Transferred the reciprocal-space model to experimental synchrotron images with minimal fine-tuning.
evidence_signal: The model achieved about 73% accuracy on atomic models and about 99% accuracy on simulated Fermi-surface projections, and the latter adapted to experimental images after fine-tuning on just two labeled frames.
watch_item: Performance on experimental data may depend on instrument quality, image alignment, and the availability of a small labeled set for fine-tuning.
paper_title: Decoding Crystallographic Surface Chirality with Machine Learning: From Atomic Geometry to Fermi Surface Projections
